studyMetadata.title: Knowing God through Jesus
studyMetadata.subtitle: A Bible Study Series for New Believers
studyMetadata.description:
A ten-session Bible study designed for people from a Muslim background who have recently come to Christ.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.
studyMetadata.author: Rick Creighton
studyMetadata.shareMessage:
Check out this Bible study app — “Knowing God through Jesus”.

Available soon on Google Play and the App Store.

theme.theme-annotation: A serene, celestial palette of deep cerulean and crisp whites. Inspired by the clarity of an azure sky and the purity of white stone, suggesting divine presence, open contemplation, and uplifting grace. Cool and clean.

elements.text: You are welcome here. Whatever it cost you to reach this point — whatever questions you are carrying, whatever fears are at the back of your mind — you have come to the right place. This series is not a debate. It is not a test. You do not need to have everything figured out before you belong. It is a place to ask honest questions, to read the Bible together, and to get to know the God who has already been at work in your life. And it is about more than what you now believe. It is about who you now are. Those two things are connected — but they are not the same thing. Over the coming chapters we will be exploring both: the truths that Christianity holds, and the identity that comes with belonging to Christ. For many people, the second of those turns out to be the deeper journey. Before we open the Bible, before we get into anything theological, we want to start with this: with you, and with welcome.

elements.question: Take a moment to read this psalm slowly before you go any further. It has been read by God’s people in every generation, in every kind of circumstance. Let the words settle.
elements.answer:
1The Lord is my shepherd, I lack nothing.

2He takes me to lush pastures, he leads me to refreshing water.

3He restores my strength. He leads me down the right paths for the sake of his reputation.

4Even when I must walk through the darkest valley, I fear no danger, for you are with me; your rod and your staff reassure me.

5You prepare a feast before me in plain sight of my enemies. You refresh my head with oil; my cup is completely full.

6Surely your goodness and faithfulness will pursue me all my days, and I will live in the Lord’s house for the rest of my life.

(NET — see context ↗)

elements.text: Your Story

Everyone has a story. You did not arrive here by accident. Something — or Someone — has been drawing you. Before we do anything else, we want to make space for that. Many people come to faith not in a calm moment, but in a difficult one — when life has raised questions that need answers, and old certainties have stopped being enough. It may have been a period of real struggle: questions that went unanswered, circumstances that fell apart, a search for something solid that nothing else could provide. If that is part of your story, you are not unusual. You are in good company. You don’t need to share more than you are comfortable with. There is no pressure. But we do want to know something of who you are.

elements.question: Where are you from, and what is one thing you love about where you grew up?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Share as much or as little as you like…

elements.question: How would you describe your journey to faith in Jesus — even if that journey still feels very new, or still feels complicated?
elements.answerPlaceholder: There are no wrong answers here…

elements.question: Is there one word that describes how you feel about being here today?

elements.text: What This Series Is
You are going to work through ten chapters together. You will read the Bible, ask questions, and think carefully — but never rush. Here is a simple map of what is ahead:

elements.answer:
Chapter 0 — Welcome: Your Story, God’s Story (this chapter)

Chapter 1 — How Can We Know God? Trusting the Bible

Chapter 2 — Who is God? The Personal God

Chapter 3 — Who is Jesus? (Part 1) Son of God and Lord

Chapter 4 — Who is Jesus? (Part 2) Understanding the Trinity

Chapter 5 — What is the Problem? Sin and the Human Heart

Chapter 6 — What Did Jesus Do? The Cross and the Resurrection

Chapter 7 — How Are We Saved? Grace, Not Works

Chapter 8 — What Does It Mean to Follow Jesus? Discipleship

Chapter 9 — New Identity, New Family: Belonging in Christ

Chapter 10 — Living with Confidence: Witness and Perseverance

elements.text:
Each chapter will follow the same pattern, so you will quickly know what to expect:

  • We will begin with an introduction to the theme
  • We will read a Bible passage together
  • We will explore it through questions — there are no trick questions, and no marks for right answers
  • We will finish with a reflection and a prayer

You do not need any prior knowledge. You do not need to have read the Bible before. All you need to bring is yourself.
Some Things Worth Knowing

elements.eyebrow: A Note on How You Got Here
elements.term: God meets people in different ways
elements.question: Tap to read before you go any further.
elements.answer:
People come to faith by very different routes. Some arrive through careful reading, argument, and honest intellectual searching. Some through the life of a person they respected. Some through a moment of crisis when everything else failed. Some through a dream or an experience they can’t fully explain, that brought an unexpected sense of peace or direction.

None of these routes is more valid than the others. God is not limited to one way of reaching people. What matters is not the road that brought you here — it is that you have arrived. You won’t be asked to defend or explain your journey. This series will simply walk forward with you from wherever you are.

elements.term: What if I ask a question that seems ignorant or offensive?
elements.question: Think about how you feel about asking questions before tapping ‘Find out’.
elements.answer: There are no ignorant or foolish questions. Many of the deepest and most important questions — about the Trinity, about whether the Bible has been corrupted, about how Jesus can be the Son of God — are questions that serious thinkers have wrestled with for centuries. If you are thinking it, write it down, and find a time to ask it. You will get the most out of these studies if you also have a chance to ask extra questions face-to-face with a leader or in a group. Every question should be taken seriously and answered as honestly as possible.

elements.term: What if I’m not sure I believe everything yet?
elements.question: Sit with that question for a moment before tapping ‘Find out’.
elements.answer: That is completely fine. This series is not a belief checklist. Faith grows. Doubt is not the opposite of faith — it is often part of the journey toward it. What matters is that you keep asking, keep reading, and keep moving forward.

elements.text: Confidentiality and Safety

Before going any further, there is something important to establish if you are doing this series in a group with other people together. (And it is best to do it in a group if you can.) If you are in a group, some of you may be in situations where your faith in Jesus is not known to your family or community, or where it carries real risk. This group needs to be a safe space.

elements.question: Please read the following agreement. Are you willing to commit to this for every session where you share with others?
elements.answerPlaceholder: I agree / I have a question about this…

elements.text: A Question to Carry

At the end of each chapter we will give you one question or thought to carry with you into the days ahead. You do not need to write an essay. You do not need to have an answer. Just let it sit with you. Here is this chapter’s question:

elements.question: What do you most hope to understand, experience, or discover through this series?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Write honestly — even if your answer is just ‘I’m not sure yet’…

elements.text: Closing Prayer

We are going to close this chapter with a simple prayer. If you are meeting in a group, and praying out loud is new or uncomfortable for you, that is completely fine — simply listen and make the words your own silently if you wish. If you are on your own you can pray out loud or silently, as you prefer.

Father God — we don’t fully understand everything yet, and that’s okay. What we know is that you have drawn each person in this room toward yourself, and we believe that is not an accident. Thank you for the journey that has brought us here — however long, however difficult, however surprising that journey has been. As we begin this series, give us open hearts and honest questions. Help us to know you — not just know about you. And let each of us find in you the belonging and security that nothing else can give.
In Jesus’ name, Amen.
See you in the next chapter. We will begin by asking one of the most important questions of all: How can we know God — and can we really trust the Bible?

chapters.chapterTitle: Who is God? — The Personal God

elements.question: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.
elements.answer:
Psalm 103
1Praise the Lord, O my soul! With all that is within me, praise his holy name! 2Praise the Lord, O my soul! Do not forget all his kind deeds! 3He is the one who forgives all your sins, who heals all your diseases, 4who delivers your life from the Pit, who crowns you with faithfulness and compassion, 5who satisfies your life with good things, so your youth is renewed like an eagle’s. 6The Lord does what is fair, and executes justice for all the oppressed. 7He revealed his faithful acts to Moses, his deeds to the Israelites. 8The Lord is compassionate and merciful, patient and replenishing in lookup. 9He does not always accuse, and does not stay angry forever. 10He does not deal with us as our sins deserve; he does not repay us as our misdeeds deserve. 11For as the skies are high above the earth, so his loyal love towers over those who fear him. 12As far as the eastern horizon is from the west, so far he removes our transgressions from us. 13As a father has compassion on his children, so the Lord has compassion on those who fear him. 14For he knows how we are formed; he remembers that we are dust. 15As for people, their days are like grass; they flourish like a flower of the field. 16When the wind blows over it, it is gone, and its place remembers it no more. 17But the Lord’s loyal love from eternity to eternity belongs to those who fear him, and his faithfulness to their grandchildren, 18to those who keep his covenant and remember to obey his precepts. 19The Lord has established his throne in heaven; his kingdom extends over everything. 20Praise the Lord, you his angels, you mighty warriors who carry out his promises and obey his words! 21Praise the Lord, all his heavenly armies, his ministers who carry out his desire! 22Praise the Lord, all that he has made, in all the regions of his kingdom! Praise the Lord, O my soul!

(NET — see context ↗)

John 1:1–18
1In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. 2The Word was with God in the beginning. 3All things were created through him, and apart from him not one thing was created that has been created. 4In him was life, and the life was the light of mankind. 5And the light shines on in the darkness, and the darkness has not mastered it. 6A man came, sent from God, whose name was John. 7He came as a witness to testify about the light, so that everyone might believe through him. 8He himself was not the light, but he came to testify about the light. 9The true light, who gives light to everyone, was coming into the world. 10He was in the world, and the world was created through him, yet the world did not recognize him. 11He came to what was his own, yet his own people did not receive him. 12But to all who have received him—those who believe in his name—he has given the right to become God’s children 13—children not born by human parents or by human desire or a husband’s decision, but by God. 14Now the Word became flesh and took up residence among us. We saw his glory—the glory of the one and only, full of grace and truth, who came from the Father. 15John testified about him and shouted out, ‘This one was the one about whom I said, “He who comes after me is greater than I am, because he existed before me.”’ 16For from his fullness we have all received, and grace after grace. 17For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ. 18No one has ever seen God. The only one, himself God, who is in closest fellowship with the Father, has made God known.

(NET — see context ↗)

Matthew 6:9–13
9So pray this way: ‘Our Father in heaven, may your name be honored, 10may your kingdom come, may your will be done on earth as it is in heaven. 11Give us today our daily bread, 12and forgive us our debts, as we ourselves have forgiven our debtors. 13And do not lead us into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one.’

(NET — see context ↗)

elements.text:
Every person has a picture of God — even those who say they do not. That picture shapes everything: how we pray, how we feel when we fail, whether we draw near or keep our distance, whether we expect love or only judgement.

For many people, that picture is of a God who is vast, sovereign, and powerful — a King, a Judge, a Creator. Those things are absolutely true. But the Bible claims something more: that this same God, the maker of heaven and earth, is also personal. He can be known, not just feared. He draws near — he doesn’t only command from a distance.

This session isn’t about replacing what you already know of God’s greatness. It is about expanding it — because the God revealed in Jesus is greater and closer than any picture we could have formed on our own.

elements.question: How would you describe your picture of God before you came to faith in Jesus? Was he close or distant? Knowable or hidden? What shaped that picture?

elements.text: Part 1 — The God Who Is Merciful and Near

Psalm 103 is one of the most beloved poems in all of Scripture. It was written by David — a man who had known both the heights of God’s blessing and the depths of his own failure — and it is a sustained meditation on the character of God. What does God actually like? What are his instincts toward human beings?

The answer, from beginning to end, is: compassion.

Do not forget all his kind deeds! <sup>3</sup>He is the one who forgives all your sins, who heals all your diseases, <sup>4</sup>who delivers your life from the Pit, who crowns you with faithfulness and compassion, <sup>5</sup>who satisfies your life with good things, so your youth is renewed like an eagle’s. <sup>6</sup>The Lord does what is fair, and executes justice for all the oppressed. <sup>7</sup>He revealed his faithful acts to Moses, his deeds to the Israelites. <sup>8</sup>The Lord is compassionate and merciful, patient and replenishing in lookup. <sup>9</sup>He does not always accuse, and does not stay angry forever. <sup>10</sup>He does not deal with us as our sins deserve; he does not repay us as our misdeeds deserve. <sup>11</sup>For as the skies are high above the earth, so his loyal love towers over those who fear him. <sup>12</sup>As far as the eastern horizon is from the west, so far he removes our transgressions from us. <sup>13</sup>As a father has compassion on his children, so the Lord has compassion on those who fear him. <sup>14</sup>For he knows how we are formed; he remembers that we are dust. <sup>15</sup>As for people, their days are like grass; they flourish like a flower of the field. <sup>16</sup>When the wind blows over it, it is gone, and its place remembers it no more. <sup>17</sup>But the Lord’s loyal love from eternity to eternity belongs to those who fear him, and his faithfulness to their grandchildren, <sup>18</sup>to those who keep his covenant and remember to obey his precepts. <sup>19</sup>The Lord has established his throne in heaven; his kingdom extends over everything. <sup>20</sup>Praise the Lord, you his angels, you mighty warriors who carry out his promises and obey his words! <sup>21</sup>Praise the Lord, all his heavenly armies, his ministers who carry out his desire! <sup>22</sup>Praise the Lord, all that he has made, in all the regions of his kingdom! elements.question: Make a list of the words and phrases in this psalm that describe God’s character. Which of these surprises you most, and why?

Verse 14 says: “He knows what we are made of; he realises we are made of clay.” What does it mean that God’s compassion is shaped by his knowledge of our weakness? How does that affect how you think about approaching him?

Verses 11–12 use two images of distance — the height of the sky above the earth, and the distance of east from west — to describe how far God removes our sins from us. Why do you think the psalmist reaches for images of distance to describe forgiveness? What is he trying to say?

elements.term: “Is the God of the Bible the same as Allah?”
elements.question: You may have wondered: are Christians and Muslims talking about the same God? Think about it before tapping to read a response.
elements.answer:
This is a question worth taking seriously rather than brushing aside. There is one Creator God — the God of Abraham, who made the heavens and the earth. In that sense, yes: Christians and Muslims are not worshipping different beings.

But the Bible makes claims about this God that go further than other traditions. It claims that God is not only Creator and King, but that he is personally relational by nature — that within the one God there is a relationship of Father, Son, and Spirit (we will explore this more in Sessions 3 and 4). And it claims that this God has acted decisively in history by coming in the person of Jesus.

The honest answer, then, is: same God — but a much fuller and more surprising revelation of who that God is. Both traditions agree on what we might call God’s substance: he is eternal, all-powerful, the Creator, without beginning or end. Where the Bible goes further is in revealing his personal character — not just that he is powerful, but that he is love at his very core, and that this love was not something he needed creation to express. It was already flowing, from eternity, within the relationship of Father, Son, and Spirit. This series exists to help you explore that revelation.

elements.text: Part 2 — The Word Who Became Flesh

Psalm 103 tells us a great deal about what God is like. But John’s Gospel goes even further: it tells us that God has made himself known in a person.

The opening of John’s Gospel — sometimes called the Prologue — is one of the most theologically dense passages in all of Scripture. It describes Jesus as the Logos — the Word — and makes a staggering claim: that this Word is not a created being, but is God himself, and has entered the world in human form.

We saw his glory—the glory of the one and only, full of grace and truth, who came from the Father. <sup>15</sup>John testified about him and shouted out, ‘This one was the one about whom I said, “He who comes after me is greater than I am, because he existed before me.”’ <sup>16</sup>For from his fullness we have all received, and grace after grace. <sup>17</sup>For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ. <sup>18</sup>No one has ever seen God. The only one, himself God, who is in closest fellowship with the Father, has made God known.

elements.question: John says that the Word “became flesh and dwelt among us.” The word translated “dwelt” literally means tabernacled — a reference to the tent in the wilderness where God’s presence rested among his people. What is John saying about Jesus with this language?

Verse 18 says: “No one has ever seen God. The only one, himself God, who is in closest fellowship with the Father, has made God known.” What does it mean that Jesus makes God known? Before Jesus, what were people left with?

John says that Jesus came to “his own” — the people of Israel — and they did not receive him (v. 11). But to those who did receive him, he gave the right to become “children of God” (v. 12). What does it mean to receive Jesus? And what does it mean to be called a child of God?

elements.text:
John 1 introduces us to something we will return to in Session 4 — the mystery of the Trinity. But even before we try to understand the structure of that mystery, we can notice what it means for us: God is not a distant, unknowable sovereign. He is the kind of God who enters the world, who “tabernacles” among his people, who can be seen and touched and heard.

The question “Can we know God personally?” is answered in Jesus: yes — because Jesus is God making himself personally known.
Part 3 — Calling God “Father”

Of all the things Jesus taught his disciples, one of the most unexpected was how to address God in prayer. He did not say: begin with God’s sovereignty. He did not say: establish your unworthiness first. He said: begin with Father.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>So pray this way: ‘Our Father in heaven, may your name be honored, <sup>10</sup>may your kingdom come, may your will be done on earth as it is in heaven. <sup>11</sup>Give us today our daily bread, <sup>12</sup>and forgive us our debts, as we ourselves have forgiven our debtors. <sup>13</sup>And do not lead us into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one.’

elements.question: To some, addressing God as “Father” feels presumptuous, or even disrespectful. What is your instinct when you hear this language? Think about it before tapping to read a response.
elements.answer:
This reaction is understandable, and worth taking seriously. In some cultures, calling God “Father” sounds like a reduction of his greatness — as if we are making him too ordinary, too familiar.

But Jesus’ intention is the opposite. The Aramaic word he would have used — Abba — is a word of deep trust and intimacy, used by a child who knows they are loved and safe. It is not casual disrespect; it is the language of secure belonging.

Crucially, this is not a title we invented for God — it is the title Jesus himself uses, and it is the title he invites us to use. To call God Father is to accept the relationship he has offered, not to claim an equality we have earned.

In saying “Our Father,” Jesus is not making a biological claim. He is revealing something about God’s character: that the God who made the universe wants his people near. He is not a distant king who tolerates subjects — he is a Father who welcomes children.

elements.question: Look at the pattern of the Lord’s Prayer. It begins with God — his name, his kingdom, his will — and only then moves to our needs. What does this structure teach us about how Jesus wants us to think about God, and about ourselves in relation to him?
Jesus teaches his disciples to pray together — “Our Father,” not “My Father.” What might it mean that belonging to God is not just a private, individual relationship, but a shared one?

elements.text: Part 4 — God’s Greatness and God’s Nearness Together

It is worth pausing to hold two things together that might feel in tension: God is infinitely great — Creator, King, Judge — and he is intimately personal, near, and knowable. The Bible does not ask us to choose between these. In fact, it insists on both.

Psalm 103 celebrates both. John 1 holds both. The Lord’s Prayer begins with the hallowing of God’s name — his holiness and greatness — and in the very same breath calls him Father.

The most remarkable claim in this session is not that God is powerful. It is that the powerful God wants to be known by you.

elements.question: Has anything in this session shifted or expanded your picture of God? If so, what, and how?

elements.text: Big Picture Truths

Here are the three key truths from this session to hold on to:

1. God is both great and near. His sovereignty, power, and holiness are never in question. But the Bible adds something: this same God draws near to those he loves. Greatness and intimacy are not in conflict — in God, they are inseparable.

2. Jesus is the fullest revelation of God. All that God is — his character, his love, his purposes — is made visible in Jesus. To look at Jesus is to see what God is like. John puts it plainly: “The only one, himself God… has made God known.”

3. God invites us to call him Father. This is not a human invention. It is the invitation of Jesus himself. To accept it is not presumption — it is faith.

elements.instruction: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements: I find it natural to think of God as personally close to me.
I feel comfortable addressing God as “Father” in prayer.
I am beginning to see God’s character more clearly through what I read in the Bible.

elements.text: Personal Reflection

elements.question: What is one image, phrase, or idea from this session — from Psalm 103, John 1, or the Lord’s Prayer — that you want to carry with you this week?
If God is truly the Father that Jesus describes — compassionate, near, and welcoming — what might that change about how you pray this week?

elements.text:
The God you have come to through Jesus is not a distant, unknowable force. He is not a God you must constantly appease, wondering if you have done enough. He is the God who, according to Psalm 103, knows you are made of clay — and loves you anyway. He is the God who, according to John 1, entered the world in person so that you might know him. He is the God who, according to Jesus, wants you to call him Father.

That is not a small thing. It is true — and we will keep returning to it throughout this series.

elements.question: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer:
Choose one of the following — or do both:

Option 1: Read Psalm 103 slowly, once each day this week. Each time, pick one phrase that strikes you and sit with it for a few minutes. What is it telling you about God?

Option 2: Use the Lord’s Prayer as a pattern for your own prayer each morning this week. Don’t just repeat the words — use each line as a prompt. “Our Father…” — speak to God as Father. “Your kingdom come…” — pray for one specific situation.

Bring something from this — a question, an observation, or a moment — to share at the start of Session 3.

elements.question: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this chapter.
elements.answer:
Mark 1:1–11
1The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God. 2As it is written in Isaiah the prophet, ‘Look, I am sending my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way, 3the voice of one shouting in the wilderness, "Prepare the way for the Lord, make his paths straight!"’ 4In the same way John the Baptist appeared in the wilderness, preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. 5And the whole Judean region and all the people of Jerusalem were going out to him and were being baptized by him in the Jordan River, confessing their sins. 6John was dressed in camel’s hair with a leather belt around his waist, and he ate locusts and wild honey. 7He proclaimed, ‘One more powerful than I am is coming after me; I am not worthy to bend down and untie the strap of his sandals. 8I baptize you with water, but he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit.’ 9Now in those days Jesus came from Nazareth in Galilee and was baptized by John in the Jordan River. 10And just as Jesus was coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens splitting apart and the Spirit descending on him like a dove. 11And a voice came from heaven: ‘You are my one dear Son; in you I take great delight.’


Mark 2:1–12
1Now after some days, when he returned to Capernaum, the news spread that he was at home. 2So many gathered that there was no longer any room, not even by the door, and he was preaching the word to them. 3Some people came bringing to him a paralytic, carried by four of them. 4When they were not able to bring him in because of the crowd, they removed the roof above Jesus. Then, after tearing it out, they lowered the stretcher the paralytic was lying on. 5When Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic, ‘Son, your sins are forgiven.’ 6Now some of the experts in the law were sitting there, turning these things over in their minds: 7’Why does this man speak this way? He is blaspheming! Who can forgive sins but God alone?’ 8Now immediately, when Jesus realized in his spirit that they were contemplating such thoughts, he said to them, ‘Why are you thinking such things in your hearts? 9Which is easier, to say to the paralytic, "Your sins are forgiven," or to say, "Get up, take your stretcher, and walk"? 10But so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins’—he said to the paralytic— 11’I tell you, get up, take your stretcher, and go home.’ 12And he got up and immediately took the stretcher and went out in front of them all, so that they were all amazed and glorified God, saying, ‘We have never seen anything like this!’


Colossians 1:15–20
15He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation, 16for all things in heaven and on earth were created by him—all things, whether visible or invisible, whether thrones or dominions, whether principalities or powers—all things were created through him and for him. 17He himself is before all things and all things are held together in him. 18He is the head of the body, the church, as well as the beginning, the firstborn from among the dead, so that he himself may become first in all things. 19For God was pleased to have all his fullness dwell in the Son 20and through him to reconcile all things to himself by making peace through the blood of his cross—through him, whether things on earth or things in heaven.


(NET — see context ↗)

elements.text:
In every generation, people have had to answer the same question about Jesus: Who is he, exactly?

Some say he was a great moral teacher. Others say he was a prophet — one of the greatest. Some say he was a revolutionary, a healer, a wise man. Islam honours him as the Messiah, born of a virgin, a worker of miracles, and one of the greatest prophets who ever lived.

But the New Testament won’t let us settle for any of these answers. Not because they are entirely wrong — but because they don’t go far enough. The Gospels press us, again and again, toward a more unsettling and more wonderful conclusion.

It is worth knowing, before we go further, that following Jesus is not adopting a foreign religion. The faith you have entered has been alive in the lands of the Middle East since the first century — long before Islam existed. You are not leaving your world for someone else’s. You are returning to something ancient.

In this chapter, we are going to let the Gospel of Mark show us who Jesus is — not through abstract argument, but through what he does and what he says. Then we will read Colossians 1, where Paul draws together the theological meaning of it all.

By the end, we will be face to face with the question Jesus himself put to his disciples: "But who do you say that I am?" (Mark 8:29)

elements.question: Before we read the passages, what words or images come to mind when you think of Jesus? Where did those impressions come from?

elements.text: Part 1 — Introduced by God: Mark 1:1–11

Mark’s Gospel is the shortest and fastest-moving of the four Gospels. There is no nativity story, no genealogy. Mark starts at full speed — and in the opening eleven verses, before Jesus has said a single word in public, three remarkable things are said about him.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God. <sup>2</sup>As it is written in Isaiah the prophet, ‘Look, I am sending my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way, <sup>3</sup>the voice of one shouting in the wilderness, "Prepare the way for the Lord, make his paths straight!"’ <sup>4</sup>In the same way John the Baptist appeared in the wilderness, preaching a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins. <sup>5</sup>And the whole Judean region and all the people of Jerusalem were going out to him and were being baptized by him in the Jordan River, confessing their sins. <sup>6</sup>John was dressed in camel’s hair with a leather belt around his waist, and he ate locusts and wild honey. <sup>7</sup>He proclaimed, ‘One more powerful than I am is coming after me; I am not worthy to bend down and untie the strap of his sandals. <sup>8</sup>I baptize you with water, but he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit.’ <sup>9</sup>Now in those days Jesus came from Nazareth in Galilee and was baptized by John in the Jordan River. <sup>10</sup>And just as Jesus was coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens splitting apart and the Spirit descending on him like a dove. <sup>11</sup>And a voice came from heaven: ‘You are my one dear Son; in you I take great delight.’

elements.question: Mark 1:1 opens with a bold claim: "The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God." Before you read any further — what do you think Mark means by calling Jesus "the Son of God"? What does that phrase suggest to you?

In verse 11, a voice from heaven speaks directly to Jesus: "You are my one dear Son; in you I take great delight." This is a remarkable moment — God himself identifying Jesus. What does this tell us about the relationship between Jesus and God, even before Jesus begins his ministry?

John the Baptist says he is not worthy to untie Jesus’ sandals — a task considered beneath even a slave’s dignity in the ancient world. What is John communicating about how he sees Jesus relative to himself? Why is this significant, given that John is himself a major prophet?

elements.text: Part 2 — Doing What Only God Can Do: Mark 2:1–12

By the time we reach Mark 2, Jesus has already been teaching with startling authority, casting out an evil spirit, and healing large numbers of sick people. But what happens next pushes the question of his identity to a sharp point.

A paralysed man is brought to Jesus by four friends — so desperate to reach him that they break through the roof. What Jesus says next stops everyone in the room.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Now after some days, when he returned to Capernaum, the news spread that he was at home. <sup>2</sup>So many gathered that there was no longer any room, not even by the door, and he was preaching the word to them. <sup>3</sup>Some people came bringing to him a paralytic, carried by four of them. <sup>4</sup>When they were not able to bring him in because of the crowd, they removed the roof above Jesus. Then, after tearing it out, they lowered the stretcher the paralytic was lying on. <sup>5</sup>When Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic, ‘Son, your sins are forgiven.’ <sup>6</sup>Now some of the experts in the law were sitting there, turning these things over in their minds: <sup>7</sup>’Why does this man speak this way? He is blaspheming! Who can forgive sins but God alone?’ <sup>8</sup>Now immediately, when Jesus realized in his spirit that they were contemplating such thoughts, he said to them, ‘Why are you thinking such things in your hearts? <sup>9</sup>Which is easier, to say to the paralytic, "Your sins are forgiven," or to say, "Get up, take your stretcher, and walk"? <sup>10</sup>But so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins’—he said to the paralytic— <sup>11</sup>’I tell you, get up, take your stretcher, and go home.’ <sup>12</sup>And he got up and immediately took the stretcher and went out in front of them all, so that they were all amazed and glorified God, saying, ‘We have never seen anything like this!’

elements.question: The teachers of the law react with outrage when Jesus forgives the man’s sins, saying: "Who can forgive sins but God alone?" (v7). They are not wrong about the logic — only God can forgive sins committed against God. So what is Jesus implying by saying what he said? What are the only two possible conclusions?

Jesus then heals the man physically, saying it is to demonstrate that "the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" (v10). He uses the title "Son of Man" — a figure from Daniel 7 to whom God gives eternal dominion over all nations. Why does he choose this title at this moment? What is he claiming?

The crowd is amazed and glorifies God (v12). The religious leaders are alarmed. Why do you think the same event produces such different responses? Which response do you find yourself more drawn to, and why?

elements.term: "The Bible never says ’I am God, worship me.’"
elements.question: You may have heard the argument that Jesus never directly claimed to be God — and that the idea was added later by his followers. What do you make of that argument before reading a response?
elements.answer:
This is one of the most common and important objections, and it deserves a careful answer rather than a dismissal.

It is true that Jesus rarely makes direct, explicit statements of the form "I am God." But the argument misunderstands how he actually communicated. Throughout the Gospels, Jesus makes claims and performs actions that, in the Jewish context of his day, could only mean one thing — and his listeners understood exactly what he was saying. That is precisely why they tried to stone him (John 10:33) and why the charge at his trial was blasphemy.

Consider what Jesus does in the Gospels:

  • He forgives sins — which, as the teachers of the law correctly understood, only God can do (Mark 2:7)
  • He accepts worship without correcting those who offer it (Matthew 14:33)
  • He speaks with his own authority, not as a prophet relaying God’s words: "You have heard it said... but I say to you" (Matthew 5)
  • He claims to be the Lord of the Sabbath — a day that belonged to God alone (Mark 2:28)
  • He says: "Before Abraham was, I am" — using the divine name (John 8:58)

The claim is made through actions, titles, and patterns of speech — not a single formula. The question is not whether the exact words appear, but whether the meaning is clear. And for his first-century Jewish audience, it was unmistakeable.

elements.text: Part 3 — The Theological Summary: Colossians 1:15–20

Mark shows us who Jesus is through what he does. Paul, writing to the church in Colossae around AD 60, steps back and gives us the theological framework — who Jesus is in relation to God, creation, and the universe itself.

What follows is one of the most elevated passages in the entire New Testament. It may originally have been an early Christian hymn — which means these words were being sung by believers within thirty years of the resurrection.

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation, <sup>16</sup>for all things in heaven and on earth were created by him—all things, whether visible or invisible, whether thrones or dominions, whether principalities or powers—all things were created through him and for him. <sup>17</sup>He himself is before all things and all things are held together in him. <sup>18</sup>He is the head of the body, the church, as well as the beginning, the firstborn from among the dead, so that he himself may become first in all things. <sup>19</sup>For God was pleased to have all his fullness dwell in the Son <sup>20</sup>and through him to reconcile all things to himself by making peace through the blood of his cross—through him, whether things on earth or things in heaven.

elements.question: Paul calls Jesus "the image of the invisible God." In the ancient world, an image was not a copy or a symbol — it embodied what it represented. A king’s image on a coin was the king’s presence and authority in that place. What does it mean for Jesus to be the image of the invisible God in this full sense?

Verses 16–17 say that all things were created through Jesus and for him — things visible and invisible, in heaven and on earth — and that he holds all things together. How does this description of Jesus compare to the picture of Jesus you had before this chapter? What expands or challenges your view?

Paul says Jesus is "the firstborn from among the dead" (v18) — meaning the first to be resurrected to an indestructible life, the beginning of the new creation. He is "first in everything." What does it mean for your life — practically, not just theologically — that Jesus holds this kind of total supremacy?

elements.text:
Notice the movement across this chapter’s three passages:

In Mark 1, God himself identifies Jesus as his Son before Jesus begins his ministry.
In Mark 2, Jesus demonstrates the authority to do what only God can do — forgive sin.
In Colossians 1, Paul draws the full conclusion: Jesus is not merely a great man whom God used, but the one through whom and for whom everything was made — God himself, made visible in a human life.

This is why the question of Jesus’ identity cannot be left open as a polite uncertainty. Everything else in the Christian faith depends on the answer.
Part 4 — The Question Jesus Asks

Halfway through the Gospel of Mark, Jesus turns to his disciples and asks two questions. The first is easy: "Who do people say I am?" The second is the one that matters: "But who do you say that I am?" (Mark 8:27–29).

That second question is not a historical curiosity. It is a live question — and it is being asked of you.

elements.question: Many people honour Jesus as a great teacher, healer, or prophet — and stop there. But the passages we have read don’t seem to leave that as a comfortable resting place. He forgives sins. He accepts worship. He claims to share glory with the Father before creation. How do you account for these things? What options does that leave you with?
What do you find most difficult about accepting the full New Testament claim about Jesus — that he is the Son of God, Lord of all creation? Is the difficulty more intellectual, or more personal?

elements.text: Big Picture Truths

Here are the three key truths from this chapter to hold on to:

1. Jesus’ identity is revealed through what he does, not only what he says. He forgives sins. He commands demons and storms. He speaks with his own authority. His actions are the claims — and they add up to one conclusion.

2. Jesus is the image of the invisible God. Paul’s language in Colossians 1 is as high as language can go: Jesus is before all things, the agent of creation, the one who holds everything together. To know Jesus is to know what God is like.

3. The question is personal, not just theological. "Who do you say that I am?" is Jesus’ own question, addressed to every person who encounters him. It cannot be answered by proxy. It requires a response from you.

elements.instruction: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements: I am becoming clearer about who Jesus is as I read the New Testament.
I find the evidence for Jesus’ divine identity in Mark’s Gospel persuasive.
I am willing to sit with the hard questions this chapter raises, rather than avoid them.

elements.text: Personal Reflection

elements.question: Having worked through this chapter, how would you now answer Jesus’ question: "But who do you say that I am?" You don’t need a perfectly formed theological answer — just an honest one.
Is there one claim about Jesus in this chapter — from Mark or from Colossians — that you want to think about more, or that you found yourself resisting? Name it honestly. What makes it difficult?

elements.text:
The Jesus of the New Testament is not a safe, manageable figure. He forgives sins that only God can forgive. He is described as the one through whom the universe was made. He asks questions that demand a personal answer.

But this same Jesus is also the one who stops for a paralysed man lowered through a roof. Who reaches out to those who are overlooked. Who, as we will see in later chapters, goes to a cross for the sake of people who have turned away from God.

His greatness and his tenderness belong together — just as we saw in Chapter 2 with God the Father. And that is no coincidence: "Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father." (John 14:9)

In Chapter 4, we will go deeper into the question of the Trinity — how Jesus relates to the Father and to the Spirit, and what it means that God is one.

elements.question: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer:
Read Mark 4–8 this week — continuing from where Chapter 1’s challenge left off.

As you read, keep a simple mental note of two things:

  1. What Jesus does — miracles, confrontations, encounters with people
  2. What people say about him — disciples, crowds, religious leaders, even demons

By the time you reach Mark 8:27–29, you will have seen enough to understand why the disciples’ answer to "Who do you say I am?" carries the weight it does.

Bring one observation — something that surprised or struck you — to share at the start of Chapter 4.

chapters.chapterTitle: Who is Jesus? (Part 2) — The Trinity Explained

elements.question: It is worth reading both passages before you begin. They record Jesus’ own words about his relationship with the Father and the Spirit — spoken on the night before his death.
elements.answer:
1‘Do not let your hearts be distressed. You believe in God; believe also in me. 2There are many dwelling places in my Father’s house. Otherwise, I would have told you, because I am going away to make ready a place for you. 3And if I go and make ready a place for you, I will come again and take you to be with me, so that where I am you may be too. 4And you know the way where I am going.’ 5Thomas said to him, ‘Lord, we don’t know where you are going. How can we know the way?’ 6Jesus replied, ‘I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me. 7If you have known me, you will know my Father too. And from now on you do know him and have seen him.’ 8Philip said to him, ‘Lord, show us the Father, and we will be content.’ 9Jesus replied, ‘Have I been with you for so long, and you have not known me, Philip? The person who has seen me has seen the Father! How can you say, “Show us the Father”? 10Do you not believe that I am in the Father, and the Father is in me? The words that I say to you, I do not speak on my own initiative, but the Father residing in me performs his miraculous deeds. 11Believe me that I am in the Father, and the Father is in me, but if you do not believe me, believe because of the miraculous deeds themselves. 12I tell you the solemn truth, the person who believes in me will perform the miraculous deeds that I am doing, and will perform greater deeds than these, because I am going to the Father. 13And I will do whatever you ask in my name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son. 14If you ask me anything in my name, I will do it. 15If you love me, you will obey my commandments. 16Then I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Advocate to be with you forever— 17the Spirit of truth, whom the world cannot accept, because it does not see him or know him. But you know him, because he resides with you and will be in you.’

1When Jesus had finished saying these things, he looked upward to heaven and said, ‘Father, the time has come. Glorify your Son, so that your Son may glorify you— 2just as you have given him authority over all humanity, so that he may give eternal life to everyone you have given him. 3Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. 4I glorified you on earth by completing the work you gave me to do. 5And now, Father, glorify me at your side with the glory I had with you before the world was created.’

(NET - see context ↗)

elements.text:
In the previous chapter we saw who Jesus is through what he does and says — forgiving sin, commanding nature, bearing the title that belongs to God alone. Now we go deeper into a question that has puzzled, challenged, and astonished people ever since: how can Jesus be fully God, if God is one?

The honest answer is: this is a mystery. No one has resolved it in a single sitting, and we won’t either. But we can do something more useful: we can let Jesus himself speak. The words we will read come from the upper room on the night before the crucifixion — the most intimate teaching Jesus ever gave his disciples. In those hours, he spoke with unusual clarity about his relationship with the Father, and about the Spirit he was about to send.

The doctrine we call the Trinity is not a formula invented by church councils. It is rooted in what Jesus said and did. So we begin where we should always begin: with Scripture.
One God — Not Three

Before we read the passages, it helps to clear away a common misunderstanding. The Christian teaching about the Trinity is frequently misunderstood — even by Christians. So let us state clearly what it is not, and what it is.

Christians do not believe in three gods. That is called tritheism, and the church has always rejected it. Christians believe that there is one God — the same one God affirmed in the Hebrew Scriptures: “Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one” (Deuteronomy 6:4). What the New Testament reveals — and what Jesus himself discloses — is that within this one God there is an eternal relationship: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Three distinct persons, one God.

This is not something Christians invented to explain a puzzle. It is something that forced itself upon the earliest followers of Jesus because of what they experienced: they were monotheists to their core, and yet they found themselves worshipping Jesus alongside the Father, and experiencing the Spirit as a living, divine presence. The Trinity is the only framework that does justice to all of that.

elements.question: When you hear the word Trinity, what image comes to mind? What do you think Christians actually believe — before we look at what the Bible says?

elements.term: “Doesn’t the Quran say the Trinity is Father, Son, and Mary?”
elements.question: The Quran’s objection to the Trinity in Al-Ma’idah 5:116 seems to be directed at a triad of Father, Son, and Mary — not Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. How do Christians respond to that?
elements.answer:
Christians respond by saying: we reject that triad too. The Bible nowhere teaches that Mary is part of the Godhead, and no mainstream Christian tradition has ever believed it. The Quran appears to be responding to a real confusion — in fact, a heresy — that existed among some Christians in 7th-century Arabia, where certain groups did venerate Mary in ways that went far beyond what Scripture taught — and may genuinely have appeared to elevate her to a divine status.

The Christian teaching is Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — not Father, Son, and Mary. On that specific point, the Quran’s objection and the Bible’s teaching are in agreement: Mary is a remarkable and honoured woman, but she is not God, and she is not part of the Trinity.

Knowing this can help clear the ground. When Muslims and Christians talk about the Trinity, they are not always talking about the same thing — and it helps to be clear about what Christians actually claim before engaging the question.

elements.text: Part 1: Jesus and the Father

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>‘Do not let your hearts be distressed. You believe in God; believe also in me. <sup>2</sup>There are many dwelling places in my Father’s house. Otherwise, I would have told you, because I am going away to make ready a place for you. <sup>3</sup>And if I go and make ready a place for you, I will come again and take you to be with me, so that where I am you may be too. <sup>4</sup>And you know the way where I am going.’ <sup>5</sup>Thomas said to him, ‘Lord, we don’t know where you are going. How can we know the way?’ <sup>6</sup>Jesus replied, ‘I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me. <sup>7</sup>If you have known me, you will know my Father too. And from now on you do know him and have seen him.’ <sup>8</sup>Philip said to him, ‘Lord, show us the Father, and we will be content.’ <sup>9</sup>Jesus replied, ‘Have I been with you for so long, and you have not known me, Philip? The person who has seen me has seen the Father! How can you say, “Show us the Father”? <sup>10</sup>Do you not believe that I am in the Father, and the Father is in me? The words that I say to you, I do not speak on my own initiative, but the Father residing in me performs his miraculous deeds. <sup>11</sup>Believe me that I am in the Father, and the Father is in me, but if you do not believe me, believe because of the miraculous deeds themselves.’

elements.question: Philip asks Jesus to show them the Father — and expects that this would be a separate, additional revelation. How does Jesus respond to that expectation, and what does his answer reveal about who he is?

elements.header: “I am in the Father and the Father is in me”
elements.question: Jesus uses the language of mutual indwelling — he is in the Father and the Father is in him. What does this language suggest about their relationship? Is this how you would describe two separate beings?

elements.text: Jesus’ words here are stunning in their directness. He does not say, “I will point you to the Father,” or “I will tell you about the Father.” He says: “The person who has seen me has seen the Father.” This is not a claim that Jesus and the Father are identical — he speaks of the Father as distinct from himself throughout these chapters. But it is a claim that Jesus is the full and final revelation of who God is. To know Jesus is to know God. No messenger or prophet has ever said anything like this — because none could.
Part 2: The Holy Spirit — A Person, Not a Force

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>‘If you love me, you will obey my commandments. <sup>16</sup>Then I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Advocate to be with you forever— <sup>17</sup>the Spirit of truth, whom the world cannot accept, because it does not see him or know him. But you know him, because he resides with you and will be in you.’

elements.question: Jesus calls the Spirit “another Advocate” — the Greek word is Paraclete, meaning one who comes alongside to help, defend, and counsel. What does calling the Spirit another Advocate imply about the Spirit’s relationship to Jesus himself?
Notice that Jesus uses personal pronouns for the Spirit — “he resides with you,” “he will be in you.” Why does it matter that the Spirit is a person and not simply an impersonal power or force?

deeper.label: Go deeper…
deeper.question: The Spirit is described as the one who will be in believers — residing within them permanently. How does the presence of the Spirit change the way we relate to God day to day? What difference does it make practically?

elements.text: For many who come from a background where God is understood primarily as transcendent and distant — above and beyond creation — the idea that God’s own Spirit lives within the believer is almost overwhelming. This is not a vague spiritual energy. This is God himself, present and personal, dwelling in those who trust in Jesus. The same Spirit that hovered over the waters at creation, that rested on the prophets, that came upon Mary — this Spirit now makes his home in every person who belongs to Christ.
Part 3: The Glory Shared Before Creation

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>When Jesus had finished saying these things, he looked upward to heaven and said, ‘Father, the time has come. Glorify your Son, so that your Son may glorify you— <sup>2</sup>just as you have given him authority over all humanity, so that he may give eternal life to everyone you have given him. <sup>3</sup>Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. <sup>4</sup>I glorified you on earth by completing the work you gave me to do. <sup>5</sup>And now, Father, glorify me at your side with the glory I had with you before the world was created.’

elements.header: “The glory I had with you before the world was created”
elements.question: Jesus prays for the Father to restore “the glory I had with you before the world was created.” What does this tell us about where Jesus came from and who he was before he was born in Bethlehem?

In verse 3, Jesus defines eternal life as knowing God — and then immediately places himself alongside the Father as the one through whom that knowing happens. What does this say about Jesus’ role in our relationship with God?

elements.text: This prayer is unlike anything recorded in any other religious text. Jesus does not pray as a prophet seeking God’s guidance. He prays as a Son who shared the Father’s glory before time began — and who is now returning to that glory after completing his mission on earth. The intimacy, the equality, the shared glory: none of this can be explained if Jesus is simply a great human messenger. This is why Christians do not simply respect Jesus. They worship him — alongside the Father and the Spirit — because this is what Jesus himself revealed as right and true.
Part 4: One Name — Three Persons

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>Therefore go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit,’
<sup>14</sup>The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all.’

elements.question: In Matthew 28:19, Jesus says to baptise in the name (singular) of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Why is it significant that Jesus uses one name — not three separate names?

Paul’s blessing in 2 Corinthians 13:14 was written around AD 55 — within 25 years of Jesus’ death and resurrection. It places Father, Son, and Spirit together naturally, without any explanation or argument. What does this suggest about how early the Trinitarian understanding was in the church?

elements.text: A Simple Framework for the Trinity

The Trinity is a mystery — but it is a revealed mystery, not an invented one. Here are three things that Christians can say clearly:

1. There is one God. Christians are not polytheists. The God of the Old Testament and the God of the New Testament are the same God. He alone is Creator, Lord, and Saviour.

2. Within that one God, there is an eternal relationship. Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are genuinely distinct — the Father is not the Son, the Son is not the Spirit — but they are not three separate gods. They are one in being, one in will, and one in love.

3. We know this because Jesus himself revealed it. The Trinity is not the result of philosophical speculation. It is the result of listening carefully to what Jesus said and did. He spoke of the Father as distinct from himself. He promised to send the Spirit. He shared glory with the Father before creation. The Trinitarian shape of God is shown in Scripture — not imposed upon it.

elements.instruction: Choose the option that best reflects where you are right now — there are no right or wrong answers here.
statements: I feel I have a clearer understanding of what the Trinity means after this chapter.
The idea that God is three persons and yet one God makes sense to me.
I find it helpful that the Trinity is based on what Jesus himself said, rather than on later church teaching.

elements.text: Common Questions

elements.term: “Isn’t the Trinity just shirk — associating partners with God?”
elements.question: This is the most important question in this chapter. What do Christians actually mean when they talk about the Trinity — and what are they not saying?
elements.answer: Shirk — associating created beings or rivals with God — is indeed a grave error, and Christians agree with that completely. But the Trinity is not a claim that God has partners or competitors. It is a claim that the one God eternally exists in a relationship of Father, Son, and Spirit. Jesus is not a second god added to the first; he is the eternal Son within the being of the one God. Christians do not worship a created being alongside God — they worship the God who has revealed himself in this Trinitarian way. The concern behind the question is right and good. The Christian answer is that the Trinity, properly understood, is not shirk — it is the revealed nature of the one God.

elements.term: “Was the Trinity invented at the Council of Nicaea in AD 325?”
elements.question: Many people believe the Trinity was a later addition to Christianity. Is that true?
elements.answer: The Council of Nicaea (AD 325) did not invent the Trinity — it defended it against those who wanted to subtract from it. The council was responding to a teacher called Arius who argued that the Son was a created being, subordinate to the Father. Nicaea said: no — the Son is of the same nature as the Father, just as Scripture teaches. The Trinitarian structure itself is found in the very earliest New Testament documents: 2 Corinthians 13:14 was written around AD 55, less than 25 years after the crucifixion. Jesus himself speaks in Trinitarian patterns throughout John 14–17. Nicaea clarified and defended what was already there. It did not create it.

elements.term: “How can God have a Son? Doesn’t that imply something physical?”
elements.question: The phrase “Son of God” can sound biological or physical. What does it actually mean?
elements.answer:
This is an understandable concern, and it is important to answer it clearly. “Son of God” does not mean that God had a child through any physical process. It is not that kind of language.

Arabic actually has two different words that are relevant and helpful here. Walad refers to a biological offspring — a child born through physical means. Ibn refers to a son in the relational, originating sense — one who comes from another, bears their nature, and stands in close relationship. The Quran’s objection to the idea of God having a son is specifically to the walad — and Christians agree completely: that is not what is meant. The Bible’s claim is of the ibn kind: Jesus is the one who originates from the very being of God, shares his nature, and stands in eternal relationship with the Father.

The Nicene Creed, written in the fourth century to address exactly this question, puts it this way: Jesus is “begotten, not made.” That phrase is worth reflecting on. Begotten means he comes from the Father — he shares the Father’s nature. Not made means he is not a creation, not something that came into existence at a point in time. He has always existed in relationship with the Father. “Son of God” names that relationship — the one who eternally proceeds from God’s own being, not the one born through any physical act.

There is something else worth noticing. The Quran itself calls Jesus kalimatuhu — “his Word” (An-Nisa 4:171). The Bible explains what that means: not a word written in a book, but the living Word who proceeded from God’s very being and entered the world as a person. “Son of God” and “Word of God” are two ways of pointing at the same truth — that Jesus comes from God, shares God’s nature, and is not a created being like any other.

elements.text:
Before we move to reflection, here is one reason why the Trinity is not just a puzzle to wrestle with — but a comfort to rest in.

The Bible says that God is love (1 John 4:8). But love, by its nature, requires someone to love. If God were a single, solitary person before creation existed, he could not have been love — he would have needed the world in order to have someone to love. That would mean God needed us to be fully himself.

The Trinity means something entirely different: God’s love did not begin when he made the world. It was already flowing, from eternity, between Father, Son, and Spirit — a relationship of self-giving love that has always existed. God did not create us because he needed love. He created us because he is love, and love overflows. Love is not an afterthought, relationships are not an afterthought. Relationships are central to who God is, and to all of reality. Love is central to who God is, and to all of reality.

This is also why Jesus could pray in John 17:24: “You loved me before the creation of the world.” The love that reached you is not new. It is eternal — and you have been welcomed into it.
Personal Reflection

The Trinity is not meant to be a puzzle to solve before you can come to God. It is a description of the God who has already come to you — in the person of Jesus, by the presence of the Spirit. You do not need to fully understand a person before you can know them. The same is true of God. Reflection is not doubt — it is the beginning of deeper knowing.

elements.question: What is the most helpful thing you have learned or seen in this chapter about who God is?
Is there something about the Trinity that still feels difficult or unsettled for you? What question would you most want to sit with this week?

elements.text: The God revealed in Jesus is not a distant, unknowable force — and he is not a simple, solitary being locked away in transcendence. He is a God of relationship, of love, of eternal self-giving. Before the world existed, Father, Son, and Spirit were already in a communion of love. And the extraordinary news of the gospel is that you have been invited into that communion. Not as an outsider. Not as a servant who must earn entry. But as one whom the Father calls his own, in the Son, by the Spirit. That is the Trinity — not a riddle, but a relationship, opened wide for you.

elements.question:
Christians throughout history have been asked: What do you believe? Who do you think Jesus is? Sometimes those questions come in a quiet conversation. Sometimes they come in a charged or difficult moment, when composing a careful answer is hard. It helps to have words already in your heart.

The Nicene Creed was written in the fourth century specifically to answer the question of who Jesus is in relation to the Father. It has been confessed by Christians across every tradition, in every country, ever since. It is not a magic formula — it is a gift: a clear, ancient answer you can carry with you.

Read it slowly. Notice what it says about the Son.
elements.answer:
We believe in one God, the Father, the Almighty, maker of heaven and earth, of all that is, seen and unseen.

We believe in one Lord, Jesus Christ, the only Son of God, eternally begotten of the Father, God from God, Light from Light, true God from true God, begotten, not made, of one Being with the Father. Through him all things were made. For us and for our salvation he came down from heaven: by the power of the Holy Spirit he became incarnate from the Virgin Mary, and was made man. For our sake he was crucified under Pontius Pilate; he suffered death and was buried. On the third day he rose again in accordance with the Scriptures; he ascended into heaven and is seated at the right hand of the Father. He will come again in glory to judge the living and the dead, and his kingdom will have no end.

We believe in the Holy Spirit, the Lord, the giver of life, who proceeds from the Father. With the Father and the Son he is worshiped and glorified. He has spoken through the Prophets.

We believe in one holy, universal, and apostolic Church. We acknowledge one baptism for the forgiveness of sins. We look for the resurrection of the dead, and the life of the world to come. Amen.

If this is new to you, you do not need to memorise it immediately. But consider returning to it this week — reading it slowly, line by line, as a prayer and a declaration. Christians in your land, and in every land, have been saying these words for seventeen centuries.

elements.question:
This week, read John 14:1–17 again slowly — as if Jesus is speaking directly to you. Notice every time he refers to the Father, to himself, and to the Spirit. As you read, ask: What is God saying to me through these words?

If you pray this week, try beginning with: “Father…” — the word Jesus himself used. Notice how it feels.

Close your time together with this prayer — or use it as a starting point for your own words.

Father, thank you that you have not left us to guess who you are. Thank you for sending your Son to show us your face. Thank you for sending your Spirit to live within us. We do not fully understand how you are one God in three persons — but we trust what Jesus has shown us. Draw us deeper into knowing you — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Amen.

chapters.chapterTitle: What is the Problem? — Sin and the Human Heart

elements.question: It is worth reading all three passages before you begin. Together they build a picture of what the Bible means by sin — and why it matters so much.
elements.answer:
Mark 7:14–23

14Then he called the crowd again and said to them, ‘Listen to me, everyone, and understand. 15There is nothing outside of a person that can defile him by going into him. Rather, it is what comes out of a person that defiles him.’ 17Now when he had left the crowd and entered the house, his disciples asked him about the parable. 18He said to them, ‘Are you so foolish? Don’t you understand that whatever goes into a person from outside cannot defile him? 19For it does not enter his heart but his stomach, and then goes out into the sewer.’ (This means all foods are clean.) 20He said, ‘What comes out of a person is what defiles him. 21For from within, out of the human heart, come evil ideas, sexual immorality, theft, murder, 22adultery, greed, evil, deceit, debauchery, envy, slander, pride, and folly. 23All these evils come from within and defile a person.’


Romans 3:9–24

9What then? Are we better off? Certainly not, for we have already charged that Jews and Greeks alike are all under sin, 10just as it is written: ‘There is no one righteous, not even one, 11there is no one who understands, there is no one who seeks God. 12All have turned away, together they have become worthless; there is no one who shows kindness, not even one.’ 13’Their throats are open graves, they deceive with their tongues, the poison of vipers is under their lips.’ 14’Their mouths are full of cursing and bitterness.’ 15’Their feet are swift to shed blood, 16ruin and misery are in their paths, 17and the way of peace they have not known.’ 18’There is no fear of God before their eyes.’ 19Now we know that whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law, so that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world may be held accountable to God. 20For no one is declared righteous before him by the works of the law, for through the law comes the knowledge of sin. 21But now apart from the law the righteousness of God (which is attested by the law and the prophets) has been disclosed— 22namely, the righteousness of God through the faithfulness of Jesus Christ for all who believe. For there is no distinction, 23for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. 24But they are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.’


Isaiah 59:1–2

1Look, the Lord’s hand is not too weak to deliver you, nor is his ear too deaf to hear you. 2But your sinful deeds have built a barrier between you and your God. Your sins have made him hide his face from you so that he does not hear.’


Mark 7 (NET — see context ↗)  |  Romans 3 (NET — see context ↗)  |  Isaiah 59 (NET — see context ↗)

elements.text:
Imagine a doctor who tells you your symptoms are minor — that you simply need to rest more and try harder. You follow the advice. But the pain keeps returning, because the doctor missed the real diagnosis. The problem was never your behaviour. It was something deeper that no amount of effort could fix.

Many people who have taken their faith seriously describe exactly this kind of restlessness — a sense that something is unresolved even after sincere religious practice. They prayed faithfully, fasted carefully, tried to obey diligently — and still something felt wrong at a level that effort couldn’t reach. If that is part of your experience, the Bible has a name for it. The problem is not a failure of devotion. It is that the diagnosis was incomplete.

That is what this chapter is for. Many of us arrive with a deeply ingrained instinct: the fundamental problem between us and God is what we do — specific sins, particular failures. And the solution, it follows, must be to do better: pray more, resist more, discipline ourselves more strictly.

But the Bible tells a different story. The problem is not simply what we do. It is what we are — what has gone wrong at the very centre of who we are. Before the gospel can be good news, we need to understand clearly what it is good news about.
Part 1: Where Does Evil Come From?

Jesus was once asked about purity — specifically about whether eating certain foods with unwashed hands made a person spiritually unclean. It was a serious religious question, and the people asking it had devoted their lives to getting the answer right. Jesus’ response surprised everyone in the room. He turned the entire question inside out.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>Then he called the crowd again and said to them, ‘Listen to me, everyone, and understand. <sup>15</sup>There is nothing outside of a person that can defile him by going into him. Rather, it is what comes out of a person that defiles him.’ <sup>17</sup>Now when he had left the crowd and entered the house, his disciples asked him about the parable. <sup>18</sup>He said to them, ‘Are you so foolish? Don’t you understand that whatever goes into a person from outside cannot defile him? <sup>19</sup>For it does not enter his heart but his stomach, and then goes out into the sewer.’ (This means all foods are clean.) <sup>20</sup>He said, ‘What comes out of a person is what defiles him. <sup>21</sup>For from within, out of the human heart, come evil ideas, sexual immorality, theft, murder, <sup>22</sup>adultery, greed, evil, deceit, debauchery, envy, slander, pride, and folly. <sup>23</sup>All these evils come from within and defile a person.’

elements.question: Jesus says that what defiles a person comes not from outside but from within — from the human heart. Why is this diagnosis more serious than simply having a list of rules we need to follow more carefully?

Look at the things Jesus says come from the human heart: evil ideas, greed, deceit, envy, pride, and more. Some of these are actions — but many of them are attitudes. What does it mean that these internal attitudes are included in Jesus’ definition of defilement?
When you hear the word sin, what comes to mind? A list of forbidden actions? A scale of serious and minor offences? Think about your instinctive definition before reading on.

elements.text: Part 2: No One is Exempt

If Jesus’ teaching in Mark 7 identifies where the problem is — inside the human heart — then Paul’s letter to the Romans identifies how far it extends. His argument is sustained and deeply uncomfortable: the problem is not limited to the obviously wicked. It reaches everyone. The religious and the irreligious. The devout and the careless. The well-intentioned and the openly rebellious.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>What then? Are we better off? Certainly not, for we have already charged that Jews and Greeks alike are all under sin, <sup>10</sup>just as it is written: ‘There is no one righteous, not even one, <sup>11</sup>there is no one who understands, there is no one who seeks God. <sup>12</sup>All have turned away, together they have become worthless; there is no one who shows kindness, not even one.’ <sup>13</sup>’Their throats are open graves, they deceive with their tongues, the poison of vipers is under their lips.’ <sup>14</sup>’Their mouths are full of cursing and bitterness.’ <sup>15</sup>’Their feet are swift to shed blood, <sup>16</sup>ruin and misery are in their paths, <sup>17</sup>and the way of peace they have not known.’ <sup>18</sup>’There is no fear of God before their eyes.’ <sup>19</sup>Now we know that whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law, so that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world may be held accountable to God. <sup>20</sup>For no one is declared righteous before him by the works of the law, for through the law comes the knowledge of sin. <sup>21</sup>But now apart from the law the righteousness of God (which is attested by the law and the prophets) has been disclosed— <sup>22</sup>namely, the righteousness of God through the faithfulness of Jesus Christ for all who believe. For there is no distinction, <sup>23</sup>for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God. <sup>24</sup>But they are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.’

elements.question: Paul quotes a string of Old Testament passages and applies them to everyone — not just the most obviously sinful. Why do you think Paul is so insistent that there is "no distinction" (verse 22)? What is he trying to close off?
Verse 20 says that the law — religious commands and rules — can diagnose sin, but cannot cure it. What does this suggest about the limits of religious observance on its own?

Paul uses the phrase "fall short of the glory of God" (verse 23). Sin is not simply breaking rules — it is a falling short of something. What do you think humanity was created to be? And what does it mean to fall short of that?

deeper.label: Go deeper…
deeper.question: Genesis 1:27 says that human beings were made in the image of God — to reflect his character, his love, his justice. If that is what we were made for, how does that change the way we understand sin? Is it primarily about rule-breaking, or about something more fundamental?

elements.text: Notice that Paul does not end at verse 23. He continues immediately into verse 24: "But they are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus." The diagnosis of sin is not the last word. It is the necessary preparation for the gospel. A doctor must name the illness before the medicine makes sense. Paul names the illness clearly and unflinchingly — precisely because the cure is extraordinary, and it must be received, not earned.
Part 3: The Separation Sin Creates

So far we have seen that sin comes from inside the human heart, and that it extends to all people without exception. But what does sin actually do? Is it simply a moral accounting problem — a debt that accumulates and must eventually be settled? The prophet Isaiah goes deeper than that.

Many people describe a religious life in which prayer felt like it was going nowhere — as though God was not listening, as though they were speaking into silence. Isaiah names that experience directly and gives it a theological explanation.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Look, the Lord’s hand is not too weak to deliver you, nor is his ear too deaf to hear you. <sup>2</sup>But your sinful deeds have built a barrier between you and your God. Your sins have made him hide his face from you so that he does not hear.’

elements.question: Isaiah makes something clear: the problem is not that God is unable to reach us, or unwilling to hear us. The problem is on our side. Sin has created a separation — an alienation. What does it feel like to be alienated from someone you were made to be close to?

Isaiah says God’s hand is "not too short to deliver" — he is fully capable of rescuing. So if God is capable and the problem lies with us, what kind of solution would actually be needed? What would have to happen for the separation to be bridged?

elements.text: A Framework: Two Ways of Understanding the Problem
